attributes.stem: A 52-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with increasing stridor and shortness of breath over four hours. He has a history of laryngeal cancer treated with radiotherapy two years ago. On examination, he appears distressed with inspiratory stridor. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 165/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. He has a short thick neck with limited range of movement. His mouth opening is restricted to two finger breadths.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate approach to airway management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute airway compromise and multiple predictors of a difficult airway including previous radiotherapy to the neck, restricted mouth opening, limited neck movement, and clinical features of obstruction (stridor). These features make conventional laryngoscopy extremely challenging and potentially dangerous.

Awake fibreoptic intubation is the safest approach in this scenario. It allows maintenance of spontaneous ventilation while securing the airway, crucial in a patient with significant airway compromise. The patient remains in control of their own airway throughout the procedure, and the technique allows careful visualization of the airway anatomy.

Rapid sequence induction would be dangerous in this situation. Once muscle relaxants are given, the ability to maintain oxygenation could be lost if intubation proves difficult, leading to a 'can't intubate, can't oxygenate' scenario.

Cricothyroidotomy is an emergency rescue technique for failed airway management. While front-of-neck access should be prepared, it should not be the first-line approach when there is time to perform awake fibreoptic intubation.

Non-invasive ventilation would not address the underlying anatomical obstruction and could potentially delay definitive airway management, leading to deterioration.

Nebulised adrenaline and observation would be inadequate given the severity of symptoms and high risk of complete airway obstruction. The underlying anatomical changes from previous radiotherapy require definitive airway management to prevent complete loss of an airway.

The key principles in managing anticipated difficult airways include careful assessment and early recognition, early senior help, maintaining spontaneous ventilation where possible, and having backup plans readily available including emergency surgical airway equipment.
learningPoints: Recognition of a potentially difficult airway is vital to reduce patient morbidity and mortality.
Risk factors for a difficult airway include pre-existing signs of obstruction (eg stridor), previous surgery or radiotherapy to the neck, restricted mouth opening and limited neck mobility.
Awake fibreoptic intubation is a safe approach for anticipated difficult airways where there is time to prepare.
references: Tsukamoto M, Hitosugi T, Yokoyama T. Awake fiberoptic nasotracheal intubation for patients with difficult airway. J Dent Anesth Pain Med. 2018 Oct;18(5):301-304. doi: 10.17245/jdapm.2018.18.5.301. Epub 2018 Oct 31. PMID: 30402550; PMCID: PMC6218389.

Kollmeier BR, Boyette LC, Beecham GB, et al. Difficult Airway. [Updated 2023 Apr 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470224/

Jung H. A comprehensive review of difficult airway management strategies for patient safety. Anesth Pain Med (Seoul). 2023 Oct;18(4):331-339. doi: 10.17085/apm.23123. Epub 2023 Oct 31. PMID: 37919917; PMCID: PMC10635845.
